perturbation.original_text: Power2Ship must provide WLI with a complete copy of its monthly airtime bill for the Units as received by the respective wireless operators (T-Mobile, Verizon, AT&T, etc.). Power2Ship is required to keep all the WLI Unit activations on every wireless network on a separate account from any other vendor's brand used by Power2Ship. Every such monthly invoice (one per operator) must be submitted to WLI within 7 days from the date it is received by Power2Ship and the invoice must include all pages listed on the invoice (example 7of 7 pages). Power2Ship's Chief Executive Officer or Chief Financial Officer must confirm in writing to WLI every quarter that the account numbers under which the WLI Units are being activated on the various networks are the only accounts under which the WLI units are activated.
perturbation.changed_text: Power2Ship must provide WLI with a complete copy of its monthly airtime bill for the Units as received by the respective wireless operators (T-Mobile, Verizon, AT&T, etc.). Power2Ship is required to keep all the WLI Unit activations on every wireless network on a separate account from any other vendor's brand used by Power2Ship. Every such monthly invoice (one per operator) must be submitted to WLI within 3 days from the date it is received by Power2Ship and the invoice must include all pages listed on the invoice (example 7of 7 pages). Power2Ship's Chief Executive Officer or Chief Financial Officer must confirm in writing to WLI every quarter that the account numbers under which the WLI Units are being activated on the various networks are the only accounts under which the WLI units are activated.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the submission deadline of monthly invoices from 7 to 3 days may contradict state laws that consider reasonable timeframes for payment or financial documentation submission.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New Jersey requires that businesses allow a reasonable time for contractual obligations.
perturbation.law_explanation: Reducing the time to submit monthly invoices to 3 days may be deemed unreasonable, violating New Jersey's UCC requirement for commercially reasonable standards in contract performance. Although the statute relates to the UCC, the general principle of 'reasonableness' could be invoked.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: New Jersey Revised Statutes Section 12A:1-302 (2022) - Variation by agreement. :: 2022 New Jersey Revised Statutes :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 N.J. Rev. Stat. 2023 N.J. Rev. Stat. 2022 N.J. Rev. Stat. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 New Jersey Revised Statutes Title 12A - Commercial Transactions Section 12A:1-302 - Variation by agreement. Universal Citation: NJ Rev Stat § 12A:1-302 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next 12A:1-302. Variation by agreement. 12A:1-302. Variation by Agreement. a. Except as otherwise provided in subsection b. of this section or elsewhere in the Uniform Commercial Code, the effect of provisions of the Uniform Commercial Code may be varied by agreement. b. The obligations of good faith, diligence, reasonableness, and care prescribed by the Uniform Commercial Code may not be disclaimed by agreement. The parties, by agreement, may determine the standards by which the performance of those obligations is to be measured if those standards are not manifestly unreasonable. Whenever the Uniform Commercial Code requires an action to be taken within a reasonable time, a time that is not manifestly unreasonable may be fixed by agreement. c. The presence in certain provisions of the Uniform Commercial Code of the phrase "unless otherwise agreed," or words of similar import, does not imply that the effect of other provisions may not be varied by agreement under this section. perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the submission deadline for monthly invoices from 7 days to 3 days, creating a potential contradiction with the principle of 'reasonableness' in contract performance, as suggested by the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically N.J. Rev. Stat. § 12A:1-302, states that obligations of reasonableness prescribed by the Uniform Commercial Code may not be disclaimed by agreement, and while parties can determine standards for performance, those standards must not be manifestly unreasonable. The change from 7 days to 3 days could be argued as manifestly unreasonable, thus contradicting the legal principle of reasonableness.

perturbation.original_text: If an invoice is past due by more than 30 days, WLI may discontinue providing technical support to Power2Ship until the invoice is paid. If Power2Ship does not make payment of any amount due and payable hereunder within 60 days of the date of invoice, WLI (without prejudice to any other remedy) shall be entitled to interest on all past due amounts at the lower of eighteen percent (18%) per annum or the highest rate permitted by law, plus reimbursement of all costs incurred in collecting such amounts, including court costs and reasonable attorney fees.
perturbation.changed_text: If an invoice is past due by more than 15 days, WLI may discontinue providing technical support to Power2Ship until the invoice is paid. If Power2Ship does not make payment of any amount due and payable hereunder within 15 days of the date of invoice, WLI (without prejudice to any other remedy) shall be entitled to interest on all past due amounts at the lower of eighteen percent (18%) per annum or the highest rate permitted by law, plus reimbursement of all costs incurred in collecting such amounts, including court costs and reasonable attorney fees.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the grace period for overdue invoices to 15 days and applying interest after 15 days may violate laws related to fair debt collection practices or usury laws, especially concerning the application of high interest rates over short periods.
perturbation.contradicted_law: The Fair Debt Collection Practices Act (FDCPA) can be interpreted to require a 'reasonable' period before actions related to debt collection can be taken.
perturbation.law_explanation: The FDCPA prohibits unfair or unconscionable means to collect or attempt to collect any debt. Shortening the payment period and aggressively pursuing collections within 15 days might be seen as an unfair or unconscionable practice, especially for business-to-business transactions, and lead to action.

perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the grace period for overdue invoices from 30 to 15 days and applies interest after 15 days. The Law Explanation states that shortening the payment period and aggressively pursuing collections within 15 days might be seen as an unfair or unconscionable practice. Given this information, and the fact that the type of perturbation is 'Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction', the modified text creates a contradiction. The scraped snippets do not directly address this situation, but the Law Explanation supports the assessment that this change could be seen as an unfair practice under debt collection laws.

perturbation.original_text: WLI may, upon written notice, terminate this Agreement at any time in the event that (a) Power2Ship defaults or otherwise fails to perform any obligation, warranty, duty, responsibility or other term or condition of this Agreement, including failure to make a payment when due and such default or failure continues unremedied for a period fifteen (15) days after such notice; (b) Power2Ship ceases permanently to carry on its present business, except as a result of a bona fide reorganization in the course of which the Agreement is transferred to a successor company of equal or greater financial resources carrying on substantially the same business; (c) Power2Ship makes an assignment for the benefit of creditors; or admits in writing its inability to pay debts as they mature; or a trustee or receiver of Power2Ship, or of any substantial part of Power2Ship's assets, is appointed by any court; or a proceeding is instituted by or against Power2Ship under any provision of the United States Bankruptcy Code or any other law affecting the rights of creditors and such proceeding is acquiesced in or is not dismissed within sixty (60) days; or (d) WLI's continued performance under this Agreement would cause it to be in violation of (i) any order of any court or regulatory agency having jurisdiction over WLI, or (ii) any law, statute, ordinance or regulation to which WLI is subject.
perturbation.changed_text: WLI may, upon written notice, terminate this Agreement at any time in the event that (a) Power2Ship defaults or otherwise fails to perform any obligation, warranty, duty, responsibility or other term or condition of this Agreement, including failure to make a payment when due and such default or failure continues unremedied for a period five (5) days after such notice; (b) Power2Ship ceases permanently to carry on its present business, except as a result of a bona fide reorganization in the course of which the Agreement is transferred to a successor company of equal or greater financial resources carrying on substantially the same business; (c) Power2Ship makes an assignment for the benefit of creditors; or admits in writing its inability to pay debts as they mature; or a trustee or receiver of Power2Ship, or of any substantial part of Power2Ship's assets, is appointed by any court; or a proceeding is instituted by or against Power2Ship under any provision of the United States Bankruptcy Code or any other law affecting the rights of creditors and such proceeding is acquiesced in or is not dismissed within sixty (60) days; or (d) WLI's continued performance under this Agreement would cause it to be in violation of (i) any order of any court or regulatory agency having jurisdiction over WLI, or (ii) any law, statute, ordinance or regulation to which WLI is subject.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the cure period for defaults to 5 days could be considered unreasonably short, potentially violating principles of good faith and fair dealing implied in contract law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New Jersey law requires contracts to be interpreted in accordance with the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.law_citation: Sons of Thunder, Inc. v. Borden, Inc., 148 N.J. 396, 420, 690 A.2d 575, 586 (1997)
perturbation.law_explanation: Reducing the cure period to 5 days might be seen as a breach of the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. A court may find that this short timeframe does not allow Power2Ship a reasonable opportunity to cure the default, especially in complex business arrangements. Although this is case law, it illustrates legal principles applicable to contract interpretation.
perturbation.justification: The original contract provides a 15-day cure period for defaults, while the changed contract reduces this to 5 days. The perturbation explanation suggests this reduction could be considered unreasonably short, potentially violating principles of good faith and fair dealing. The law explanation supports this by stating a court might find the 5-day timeframe unreasonable, especially in complex business arrangements. The 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition states that inconsistencies arise when time-sensitive obligations in a contract do not align with legal requirements, and a legal contradiction happens when a contract sets a deadline or requirement that violates federal or state law, making the contractual terms unenforceable or illegal. While there are no specific law snippets to point to, the law explanation highlights that a 5 day cure period could be seen as bad faith, which violates legal principles of fair dealing in contracts, creating a contradiction.
